coredata.dc:title: Sensitive and selective spectrofluorimetric determination of clonazepam using nitrogen-doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Ghafarloo, Ali
Emamali Sabzi, Reza
Samadi, Nasser
Hamishehkar, Hamed
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel fluorescence sensor using Carbon Dots (CDs) doped with nitrogen as sensing materials was introduced for fast and selective determination of Clonazepam. Ammonia was used as a nitrogen source to dope CDs with nitrogen atoms. It was observed that, the fluorescence intensity of CDs increased intensively after doping. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased by the increase of Clonazepam concentration and a blue shift was observed in maximum wavelength to lower wavelengths of about 15 nm. CDs showed blue fluorescence at 430 nm by excitation at 340 nm. This fluorescent sensor was successfully used to quantify Clonazepam in the linear ranges 5 × 10−8M to 7 × 10-6M with a limit of detection equal to 1.2 × 10−8M. The proposed method was successfully applied for determination of Clonazepam in plasma and tablet dosages offering the some advantages such as high sensitivity and selectivity toward similar compounds, ease of operation, low cost, high quantum yield of 31% and rapid response time.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Sensor
Carbon dots
Clonazepam

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for optimization of fluorescence measurements

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of CDs synthesis and quenching mechanism.

Absorption and emission spectra of CDs (a) UV–vis absorption of CDs in phosphate buffer solution (b) Fluorescence intensity of CDs and CDs + Clonazepam: (right cell) image of CDs solution and (left cell) CDs + Clonazepam solution under UV-cabinet; (c) Fluorescence spectra of CDs vs. different excitation wavelength at room temperature (d) FTIR spectra of CDs.
CDs: (a) TEM image (b) and their size distribution.

(a) Fluorescence emission at various concentration of CDs, (b) Fluorescence intensity of CDs in the absence and presence of (2.5μM) Clonazepam at various pH levels, (c) Sensor response time after adding (2.5μM) Clonazepam to fluorescence cell (c) and stability of CDs within 9 months.
Individual Interference of Different Substances in Determination of Clonazepam.
Fluorescence response of Clonazepam and mixtures of interfering substances in absence and presence of Clonazepam.

(a) fluorescence spectra of CDs in the presence of various concentration of Clonazepam from top to bottom (5.0 × 10−8 to 7.0 × 10-6 M) in phosphate buffer (pH = 6. 8) solution, (b) calibration curve (c) and Schematic illustration of Clonazepam detection based on CDs fluorescence sensor.
Determination of Clonazepam in human plasma sample using the proposed sensor.
Determination of Clonazepam (1 mg) in pharmaceutical products of Clonazepam using the proposed sensor.
comparison of proposed sensor with others spectrophotometric and spectrofluorimetric approach.

head.ce:title: Sensitive and selective spectrofluorimetric determination of clonazepam using nitrogen-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Urmia University, Urmia, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Urmia University, Urmia, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of CDs synthesis and quenching mechanism

In this study, a novel fluorescence sensor using Carbon Dots (CDs) doped with nitrogen as sensing materials was introduced for fast and selective determination of Clonazepam. Ammonia was used as a nitrogen source to dope CDs with nitrogen atoms. It was observed that, the fluorescence intensity of CDs increased intensively after doping. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased by the increase of Clonazepam concentration and a blue shift was observed in maximum wavelength to lower wavelengths of about 15 nm. CDs showed blue fluorescence at 430 nm by excitation at 340 nm. This fluorescent sensor was successfully used to quantify Clonazepam in the linear ranges 5 × 10−8M to 7 × 10-6M with a limit of detection equal to 1.2 × 10−8M. The proposed method was successfully applied for determination of Clonazepam in plasma and tablet dosages offering the some advantages such as high sensitivity and selectivity toward similar compounds, ease of operation, low cost, high quantum yield of 31% and rapid response time.

ce:section.ce:para: Clonazepam as a benzodiazepine is commonly prescribed for alleviating the anxiety and epilepticus as well as preoperative sedation and management of nausea. Several desirable effects of clonazepam such as muscle relaxant, sedative, and anti-anxiety activities may become undesirable in certain situations, for example sedation and low blood pressure can sometimes be harmful. Clonazepam acts in a dose-dependent manner meaning that higher doses exert more severe effects and side effects including fatigue, lethargy, and drowsiness [1,2]. Development of fast, inexpensive, selective and sensitive analytical methodologies for routine determination of Clonazepam in pharmaceutical formulations and biological samples is of significant importance because of extensive usage of this drug.

Several analytical techniques have been reported for determination of Clonazepam including gas chromatography with electron capture detection (GC-ECD) [3,4], LC–MS [5], HPLC [6–9], GC–MS [10,11] spectrophotometry [12–14], chemiluminescence [15] and electrochemical methods such as potentiometry [16] and voltammetry [17–19]. These methods have high precision and sensitivity but some disadvantages such as the requirement for expensive materials and equipments, difficult sample preparation procedures and time consumption making many of these methods inadequate for routine analyses. Spectrofluorimetric methods have advantages over other analytical techniques such as high selectivity and sensitivity compared to UV–vis absorption methods and time and cost-effective procedures compared to chromatographic methods in which there are extra sample preparation steps each using expensive materials. To the best of our knowledge, there are no similar reports on the determination of Clonazepam by spectroflourimetric methods using CD probes.

Different fluorescent probes such as organic dyes and metal quantum dots have been used in determination of drugs, biological molecules and metal cations [20–22]. Most organic fluorophores such as synthetic ligands [23,24] are unstable in aqueous solutions and suffer from photobleaching which leads to irreproducible results. Metal QDs address these disadvantages, but other drawbacks including difficult synthesis, relatively high toxicity and reactivity and their low biocompatibility limit their widespread use especially in biological applications [25].

CDs are novel carbonaceous nanomaterials with diameters of less than 10 nm which exhibit excellent optical properties, high photostability, very high aqueous solubility and stability, low toxicity, high biocompatibility, low cost, and simple synthesis methods providing potential application possibilities in sensing and imaging [26,27]. CDs as fluorescent probes have been used for determination of metal cations [28–36], amino acids [37–39] and drugs [40–53]. Most CDs have relatively low fluorescence intensity compared to conventional semiconductor quantum dots, even after surface functionalization. Recently, doping approach with heteroatoms have enhanced their fluorescent properties and influenced their emission efficiency, also making them more efficient probes for drug detection and determination. For example, doping with non-metals like N, P, and S and co-doping with multiplex heteroatoms N–S and N–P have been actively considered by the researchers. Among these, N-doping employed more than others due to close similarity between C and N. The nitrogen atom injects electrons into the CDs internal structure and changes the electronic environment which improves the properties of fluorescence effectively [25].

In this study, the N-doped CDs were synthesized using citric acid and used for the selective and sensitive determination of Clonazepam. The sensor offers a rapid and selective response to Clonazepam in wide ranges of concentrations compared to other structurally similar compounds. Under optimal conditions, the intensity of fluorescence in the prepared CDs gradually decreases by increasing the concentration of Clonazepam. The proposed sensor was successfully employed for determination of Clonazepam in plasma samples and pharmaceutical formulations.

Clonazepam, Lorazepam, Alprazolam and Chlorodiazepoxide (pure powders, 99.0–101.0%) were purchased from FIS Company. Ammonia solution 25% and anhydrous citric acid were purchased from Merck Company. HCl solution (36–38 wt %) and KOH flakes were also obtained from Merck Company. All other chemical reagents used in these experiments were of the highest purity and supplied from Merck Company. Experiments were conducted using PBS buffer (pH = 6.8, 0.1 M potassium phosphate) prepared as follows. Briefly, 27.22 g of KH2PO4 was dissolved in water and diluted with water to 1000 ml. 100 ml of the solution was placed in a 200-ml volumetric flask, and water was added to near volume indicator line, pH was adjusted at 6.8 by pH-meter using NaOH solution, and finally water was added to volume indicator line. Methanol was used to dissolve benzodiazepines and distilled water was used to prepare CDs in phosphate buffer solution and all other solutions in this study.

CDs were synthesized with citric acid and ammonia by pyrolysis at high temperatures according to a prior report [54] with slight modification as follows: 1 g of anhydrous citric acid was accurately weighed, transferred into 10 ml of ammonia 5% solution, and stirred on a stirrer until complete dissolution. The obtained solution was poured to a test tube, transferred to an electric furnace fixed at 230 °C and heated for 2 h. A black viscous solution was obtained, dissolved in water, and centrifuged at 8000 rpm to remove large particles. Then, it was dialyzed using a semi-permeable membrane (with MW cutoff of 3000) to remove impurities and unreacted materials. After dialysis, a brown solution was obtained. The solution was freeze dried and used in the following experiments Fig. 1 .

UV-Vis spectra were recorded using a spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The functional groups of CDs were determined on a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR-Shimadzu, Japan) by preparing KBr pellets. The fluorescence spectra of all solutions were recorded on a JASCO FP-750 spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan) equipped with a 150-W xenon lamp using a quartz cell with light path length of 1 cm. The morphology of CDs was determined using a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Leo 906, Zeiss, Germany) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. pH levels of the samples were measured using a pH-meter (Mettler Toledo).

The UV–vis spectra of CDs are shown in Fig. 2 a. The spectra depicted maximum absorption bands at about 230 and 327 nm. The peak at about 230 nm was related to π-π* transition of aromatic CC band, similar to polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon CC systems. The peak at about 327 nm appeared due to n- π* transition of CO band. According to fluorescence spectrum, maximum emission at 440 nm was observed when excited at 340 nm and displayed a strong blue color under UV lamp at 364 nm. Fig. 2b depicts the fluorescence spectra of CDs under optimal conditions in phosphate buffer (pH = 6.8) solution. The strong fluorescence intensities of CDs decreased with the addition of Clonazepam as demonstrated under a UV lamp cabinet. After addition of Clonazepam, the blue color of CDs disappeared. CDs also exhibit excitation-dependence fluorescence behavior. By increasing the excitation wavelength with 10 nm intervals at wavelength range of 290 nm to 410 nm, fluorescence emission spectra shifted to longer wavelengths and the intensities of fluorescence spectra decreased Fig. 2c. The fluorescence quantum yield of CDs was calculated using quinine sulfate as a standard. The FTIR spectrum is depicted in Fig. 2d enabled the determination of surface functional groups of CDs. According to IR spectra, the strong and broad peak at about 3400 cm−1 corresponded with OH stretched surface attached to phenolic group. The peaks at 2930 and 2854 cm−1 corresponded with CH stretching vibrations of the aromatic compound. The strong band at about 1716 cm−1 was assigned to CO stretched carboxylic acid or other carbonyl functional groups attached to the surface. The peaks at about 1380 and 3243 cm−1 corresponded with C–N and NH stretching vibrations, respectively. The results obtained from IR spectra showed that, the surfaces of CDs included hydrophilic groups (hydroxyl, carboxylic acid, and amine) which increased its solubility in water. CDs were well dispersed in water and its solution was clear. Solution preparation was done without further ultrasonic dispersion. Fig. 3 a shows the TEM image of the CDs. As shown in this figure; CDs were well-separated with no aggregation. The diameter of CDs ranged from 3 to 9 nm indicating the successful formation of CDs. TEM images also showed that, the CDs were round in shape Fig. 3b.

The quantum yield of CDs was calculated using quinine sulphate (QY = 0.54 in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4) [43,51]. At first, solutions of CDs and quinine sulfate were prepared and diluted until obtaining absorbance less than 0.1 to minimize the re-absorption effects. Then the emissions of both were recorded at 340 nm excitation wavelength and 3 nm slit. Finally, the quantum yield was calculated based on the following equation.
Q S
=
Q R
× (
I S
I R
) × (
A R
A S
) ×
(
η S
η R
)
2
Where the Q represents the quantum yield, I represents the area under the fluorescence curve of sample, A represents the absorbance of the reference and ŋ represents the refractive index. Refractive index of water is 1.33 and the subscript R refers to reference standard and S refers to the sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for optimization of fluorescence measurements
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, the preliminary studies of the proposed fluorescence sensor for optimal condition are described. First, fluorescence intensity of CDs was scrutinized. To do so, 100 mg of CDs was accurately weighted, transferred to a 100-ml volumetric flask, 20 ml of water added and the mixture was shaken until completely dissolved and homogenous solution obtained. Then, the final volume of the mixture was adjusted to volume indicator line using water to obtain stock solution. The fluorescence intensity of the solution was measured. Different concentrations of CDs were prepared and their fluorescence intensity was measured at slit 5 nm. It was observed that the CDs emission was linear and proportional with increasing concentration of CDs but it was deviated from linear line in concentration above 0.0002 mg/ml. The lower the concentration, the lesser the linear range would be and the higher the concentration, the greater the self-absorbance of emission light would be and causing self-quenching of CDs. So according to the Fig. 4 a, maximum emission in 0.0002 mg/ml concentration was selected as next measurement which also obtained more CDs active site [50]. Second, the interaction of CDs with several benzodiazepines was studied by spectrofluorimetry, and the results showed that, only Clonazepam quenched the fluorescence intensity while others had negligible effect on CDs fluorescence emission. Concomitantly, the influence of pH in the ranges 2.0 to 12.0 on the structure of CDs was investigated by spectrofluorimetric measurements. Several CD solutions with different pH levels were prepared and their intensities were measured in the absence and presence of Clonazepam. Results showed that, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was invariable in the ranges 4 to 10 and the signal ratio was at maximum in this ranges between two experiments. However, outside this range, fluorescence intensity depended on pH level. Lower and higher pH values decreased signal intensity and signal ratio. In the presence of Clonazepam, there was good steady fluorescence intensity at pH values of 4 to 10 (Fig. 4b). Therefore, the next measurement was performed at pH of 6.8 because at this pH level, the fluorescence intensity was constant and approached biological moiety. Moreover, signal ratio was maximal and higher sensitivity, and therefore lower detection limit was obtained.

The response time was studied and results showed that after first measurement no significant change was found in fluorescence quenching (Fig.4c). So, it was confirmed that, this method could be applied for fast determination of Clonazepam without any delay and by saving the time. The stability of CDs during the research was investigated within 9 months by measuring the fluorescent intensity. The results indicated a negligible decrease in the intensity, confirming the stability of CDs for long period of time as shown in Fig. 4(d).

Increased sensitivity in Clonazepam measurement is very important because the level of Clonazepam in pharmaceutical products and blood samples is comparatively low and there is more interference during the determination of Clonazepam in a real sample with a complicated matrix.

Selectivity is among the most important properties of a sensor, which is the relative response of the sensor to primary analyte over other ions and molecules of the solution [24]. To study the selectivity of this method, the fluorescence response of CDs to potential interfering substances was investigated in plasma and pharmaceutical formulations including cations, amino acids, and other small molecules. Under optimal conditions, the cations Cu2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, and Zn2+ were added to CDs system which showed small effects on CDs fluorescence. Other potential interfering substances including glucose, urea, tryptophan, and glutathione had negligible effects on the fluorescence enhancing or quenching of CDs. Other Benzodiazepines such as Chlorodiazepoxide, Lorazepam and Alprazolam also had little effects on fluorescence intensity.

Selectivity studies were carried out under optimal conditions. First, every interfering substance was separately added to the CD solution and their interactions with CDs were determined Fig. 5 . The results indicated that, only Clonazepam selectively decreased the fluorescence of CDs. Second, mixtures of interfering substances were introduced to CDs in the presence and absence of Clonazepam Fig. 6 . The results indicate selective interaction between Clonazepam and CDs. The presence of nitro functional group in Clonazepam may induce some interactions with surface functional groups of CDs and influence on the excited-state electron configuration resulting in the fluorescence quenching, while other structurally similar compounds like Alprazolam, Lorazepam and Chlorodiazepoxide did not exhibit such property.

The CDs fluorescence spectra were investigated upon successive addition of Clonazepam to the measurement cell and recording spectra after each addition. As depicted in Fig. 7 a, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was gradually decreased by increasing Clonazepam concentration and had a linear range of from 5 × 10−8 to 7.0 × 10-6 with low limit of detection equal to 12 nM. Fluorescence spectra showed that, by the addition of Clonazepam, the maximum wavelength of CDs shifted to lower wavelengths (blue shift) by about 15 nm (430 nm to 415 nm). This shift indicated that Clonazepam could attach to surface functional groups and change the surface properties of CDs. According to the results obtained in the previous section showing that the same structure of Clonazepam had no effect on CDs intensity, it can be inferred that nitro group in Clonazepam may attach to functional groups on the surface of CDs and transfer electron from excited-state to the aromatic structure of Clonazepam and cause non-radiative relaxation which quench CDs emission.

To obtain a clear view on selectivity, high selectivity obtained for Clonazepam by this method verified the excellent potential of the proposed sensor to monitoring Clonazepam at trace concentrations in plasma and Clonazepam tablet samples.

A comparison was carried out with other spectrophotometric and spectrofluorimetric methods. As tabulated in Table 3 , the proposed sensor offers lower detection limit compared to photometric methods and wider linear range compared to both photometric and fluorimetric methods. Beside, this method is rapid, simple and cost-effectiveness compared to mentioned methods.

In the present study, a novel fluorescence sensor was successfully fabricated using CDs as probe for selective and sensitive detection of Clonazepam in aqueous solutions. The fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased as Clonazepam concentration increased attributed to the interaction between CDs and Clonazepam. The remarkable properties of this method using CDs include low toxicity, stable photoluminescence, excellent solubility, chemically inertness and high quantum yield. These led to the design of a simple, rapid, selective, sensitive, and low-cost method for determination of Clonazepam in aqueous solutions.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Urmia University, Urmia, Iran, and the Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pharmacokinetics and side-effects of clonazepam and its 7-amino-metabolite in man
Eur. J. Clin. Pharmacol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: O. Sjö, E. Hvidberg, J. Naestoft, M. Lund, Pharmacokinetics and side-effects of clonazepam and its 7-amino-metabolite in man, European journal of clinical pharmacology, 8 (1975) 249-254.

Drugs

J. Pharm. Sci.

J. Chromatogr. A

Forensic Sci. Int.

J. Chromatogr. B Biomed. Sci. Appl.

Chromatographia

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Habibi, M. Jahanbakhshi, Silver nanoparticles/multi walled carbon nanotubes nanocomposite modified electrode: Voltammetric determination of clonazepam, Electrochimica Acta, 118 (2014) 10-17.
